coredata.dc:title: Interlayered thin-film nanocomposite membrane with synergetic effect of COFs interlayer and GQDs incorporation for organic solvent nanofiltration
coredata.dc:creator: Li, Shuxuan
Yin, Yating
Liu, Shaoxiao
Li, Honghai
Su, Baowei
Han, Lihui
Gao, Xueli
Gao, Congjie
coredata.dc:description: The separation performance of current organic solvent nanofiltration (OSN) membranes are still not satisfied. In this work, an ultra-thin TpHz covalent organic frameworks (COFs) interlayer was in situ constructed in advance on polyimide substrate, then an ultra-thin polyamide separation layer incorporated with amino-functionalized graphene quantum dots (af-GQDs) was constructed via interfacial polymerization (IP). A high performance interlayered thin-film nanocomposite (iTFN) OSN membrane with integrally covalent bonding structure, ultra-thin and ultra-smooth surface was obtained after post crosslinking and activation. The effect of ultra-low concentrations for the polyamide reaction monomers, the COFs monomer Tp and the doped af-GQDs nanoparticle reveals that they could help to form a very thin (7–8 nm in thickness) and defect-free skin layer with very smooth surface (about 2.7 nm in roughness). The iTFN OSN membrane shows excellent solvent permeance and solute selectivity, with an ethanol permeance reaching 97.6 L m−2 h−1 MPa−1 and an RDB rejection higher than 99%. After a 180 h long-term filtration with 100 mg L−1 RB/DMF solution, it remained an RB rejection of higher than 99%, demonstrating its superior resistance to strong polar solvent. Our work paves a unique way for the fabrication of novel OSN membrane with both in situ constructed nanomaterials interlayer and nanomaterials incorporated in skin layer.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Interlayer
Covalent organic frameworks (COFs)
Amino-functionalized graphene quantum dots (af-GQDs)
Organic solvent nanofiltration (OSN)
Interfacial polymerization (IP)

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Fabrication process of TpHz COFs interlayer
Fabrication process of the iTFN OSN membrane
Surface chemistry of the TFC and iTFN membranes

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Schematic fabrication process of the iTFN OSN membranes.
Possible chemical reactions in the membrane preparation process.
XPS spectra of C1s, O1s and N1s for TFC and iTFN membranes.
Influence of MPD concentration on separation performance of the iTFN-c1-0.15-25-50 OSN membranes.
Surface SEM images of the iTFN-c1-0.15-25-50 membranes, MPD concentration: (a) 1.0 wt%; (b) 0.5 wt%; (c) 0.025 wt%.
Influence of TMC concentration on the separation performance of the iTFN-0.025-c2-25-50 membranes.
The TEM images of OSN membranes: (a) TFN-0.025-0.05-50 membrane; (b) iTFN-0.025-0.05-15-50 membrane.
Influence of Tp concentration on the separation performance of the iTFN-0.025-0.05-c3-50 membranes.
Surface AFM images of (a) iTFC-0.025-0.05-15 membrane, (b) iTFN-0.025-0.05-15-50 membrane and (c)iTFN-0.025-0.05-15-100 membrane.
The effect of af-GQDs content on the separation performance of the iTFN-0.025-0.05-15-c4 membranes.
Pure solvent permeance of the iTFN-0.025-0.05-15-50 membrane.
The rejections of the iTFN-0.025-0.05-15-50 OSN membrane for PEGs in water with different molecular weight.
The organic solvent resistance performance test of the iTFN-0.025-0.05-15-50 OSN membrane immersing in DMF at 80 °C.
The long -time filtration stability performance of the iTFN-0.025-0.05-15-50 OSN membrane at 0.5 MPa and 25 °C.
The content of different chemical groups on the membrane surface.
The separation performance of OSN membranes in this work and other OSN membrane in the state-of-the-art literature works.

head.ce:title: Interlayered thin-film nanocomposite membrane with synergetic effect of COFs interlayer and GQDs incorporation for organic solvent nanofiltration

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Key Laboratory of Marine Chemistry Theory and Technology (Ocean University of China)

ce:footnote.ce:note-para: First authors, Shuxuan Li and Yating Yin contribute equally.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: The separation performance of current organic solvent nanofiltration (OSN) membranes are still not satisfied. In this work, an ultra-thin TpHz covalent organic frameworks (COFs) interlayer was in situ constructed in advance on polyimide substrate, then an ultra-thin polyamide separation layer incorporated with amino-functionalized graphene quantum dots (af-GQDs) was constructed via interfacial polymerization (IP). A high performance interlayered thin-film nanocomposite (iTFN) OSN membrane with integrally covalent bonding structure, ultra-thin and ultra-smooth surface was obtained after post crosslinking and activation. The effect of ultra-low concentrations for the polyamide reaction monomers, the COFs monomer Tp and the doped af-GQDs nanoparticle reveals that they could help to form a very thin (7–8 nm in thickness) and defect-free skin layer with very smooth surface (about 2.7 nm in roughness). The iTFN OSN membrane shows excellent solvent permeance and solute selectivity, with an ethanol permeance reaching 97.6 L m−2 h−1 MPa−1 and an RDB rejection higher than 99%. After a 180 h long-term filtration with 100 mg L−1 RB/DMF solution, it remained an RB rejection of higher than 99%, demonstrating its superior resistance to strong polar solvent. Our work paves a unique way for the fabrication of novel OSN membrane with both in situ constructed nanomaterials interlayer and nanomaterials incorporated in skin layer.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Organic solvents have been widely used in chemical and other correlative industries. According to the report [1], organic solvents account for 80%–90% by weight of the total chemical reagents used in pharmaceutical and fine chemical processing industries, while their reuse rate is less than 50%. The separation process of organic solvent systems accounts for about 40%–70% of the investment and operating charges in chemical and pharmaceutical industries [2]. Hence, the separation and purification of organic solution systems in harsh environment is a major need for energy saving and emission reduction, efficient resource utilization, environmental protection and sustainable economic development. Nevertheless, it is also a major challenge in the field of membrane separation.

Organic solvent nanofiltration (OSN) process has an energy consumption of only about one tenth that of traditional distillation process [2], and is an emerging advanced membrane technology for the separation and purification of organic solvent system. The heart of OSN process is OSN membrane. Noteworthily, most OSN membranes nowadays are thin-film composite (TFC) fabricated via the well-known interfacial polymerization (IP) method which was invented by Cadotte [3]. The IP reaction possesses the characteristics of self-completing and self-limiting. Usually, the composite membrane fabricated via this method has thin and less defective skin layer with stable structure, high solute rejection and high solvent permeance [4]. Prof. Livingston's group [5–8] and Prof. Vankelecom's group [9–11] carried out a lot of work in the preparation of TFC OSN membranes. All these works prove that TFC configuration is much suitable for OSN membranes. However, up to now, most TFC OSN membranes reported in literatures still suffer from relatively low solvent permeance while they maintain a reasonable solute rejection, or relatively poor solute rejection while they maintain a very large solvent permeance, namely, the “trade-off” effect.

Nanomaterials have been considered as a kind of promising additives that could mitigate the “trade-off” relation of TFC membranes [12]. Following Hoek's work [13], many researchers added nanomaterials as fillers into the skin layer to realize the improvement of the water or solvent permeance of thin-film nanocomposite (TFN) membranes such as reverse osmosis, nanofiltration (NF) and OSN membranes [14–16]. The widely used nanomaterials include graphene quantum dots (GQDs) [15,17], graphene oxide (GO) [18], covalent organic frameworks (COFs) [19,20], as well as metal organic frameworks (MOFs) [21]. Li et al. [15] doped GQDs nanomaterials in the aqueous solution of m-Phenylenediamine (MPD) and obtained a monolithically crosslinked TFN OSN membrane with greatly enhanced ethanol permeance and slightly increased rhodamine B (RDB 479 Da) rejection. Zhou et al. [22] introduced hypercrosslinked polymer additives into the selective layer and successfully improved the separation performance and stability of TFN OSN membranes. Prof. Chung's group [20] fabricated an OSN membrane with Janus-like characteristics by in-situ growth COFs on crosslinked polyimide (PI) hollow fiber substrate, and achieved greatly improved solute rejection and super-high permeances for both polar and nonpolar solvents. All these works prove enhanced separation performance with the aid of nanomaterials introduction.

However, although the preparation of TFN membranes by doping nanomaterials in the aqueous phase or organic phase solution has been studied extensively, this method still suffers from poor dispersion of nanomaterials in the monomer solution, which could cause defects in the membrane skin, and meanwhile, could weaken the function of nanomaterials during the fabrication process and the subsequent filtration process as well.

Recently, researchers effectively improved the comprehensive performance of TFC membranes by introducing nanomaterial interlayers which was first invented by Prof. Livingston [8]. Many research works have been carried out on this subject, and the fabricated membranes are usually referred to as thin-film nanocomposite (TFN) [23–25], or thin-film nanocomposite with interlayer (TFNi) [26]. Zha et al. [27] fabricated three kinds of OSN membranes with interlayers of porous TpTD COFs, chained polyaniline and networked polydopamine, respectively. All these interlayered OSN membranes exhibited great long-term stability, anti-fouling performance and chemical resistance. Prof. Wang's group [28] prepared a TFC OSN membrane with a functional interlayer resulting from the polyelectrolyte-based layer-by-layer self-assembly. Compared to the interlayer-free OSN membrane, the prepared membrane had a smoother selective layer with higher crosslinking degree and an improved organic dye rejection. Yang et al. [29] prepared an OSN membrane with an ultra-thin TpPa COFs interlayer, and achieved an RDB rejection of above 99%, an ethanol permeance of 60 L m−2 h−1 MPa−1, together with good resistance to organic solvents. These works give us a great indication that interlayer could help to construct nanocomposite membranes with much better performance.

As the configuration of nanomaterials-interlayered TFC membranes is not the same as that of TFN membranes in which nanomaterials are incorporated in the skin layer, we term the above nanomaterials-interlayered thin-film composite as iTFC. Till now, the thickness of the skin layer of iTFC membrane has been decreased greatly down to less than 20 nm [25] or even lower than 10 nm [8] due to the aid of the interlayer, and the hydraulic resistance of the skin layer has been greatly decreased. It is very difficult to decrease further the thickness of the skin for the sake of high solute rejection. Nevertheless, as the ultra-thin skin layer prepared with the aid of the nanomaterials interlayer is dense and usually very smooth, the iTFC OSN membranes still show a relatively low solvent permeance.

We consider that the above iTFC membranes could possess a looser skin layer with the aid of nanomaterials incorporated therein, so that the solvent resistance could be decreased effectively. In this work, we used ultra-low concentration for both hydrazine hydrate (Hz) and 2,4,6-trihydroxy-1,3,5-benzenetricarbaldehyde (Tp) to construct in-situ an interlayer of TpHz COFs with intrinsic aperture of 0.8 nm on the surface of PI substrate, and then we constructed thereon an ultra-thin separation layer incorporated with af-GQDs nanoparticles via IP process. For the first time, we fabricated a kind of TFN membrane with nano-structured interlayer together with nanomaterials incorporated in the skin layer, and termed this novel structure as iTFN. We successfully engineered the iTFN OSN membrane via synergistically modulating the interlayer and the separation layer, and subsequent crosslinking and activation processes. In this way, we aim to obtain a kind of iTFN OSN membrane with integrally covalent bonding structure, ultra-thin and ultra-smooth separation layer, domain-limited mass transfer function and high performance for efficient organic solutions treatment. This novel preparation process is expected to provide a new idea for high performance OSN membranes preparation.

The experimental materials used are shown in Table 1 , all chemicals were used directly without further purification. The synthesis of the af-GQDs nanosheets is the same as our previous work [30]. The experimental instruments used in the characterization of the prepared membranes are shown in the supplementary materials.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fabrication process of TpHz COFs interlayer
ce:section.ce:para: The preparation method of PI substrate was the same as our previous research work [31]. The obtained PI substrate was fixed on a glass plate, and then the substrate surface was soaked in the aqueous Hz solution for 8 s. After removing the aqueous solution and drying the substrate in air at room temperature for about 1 min, the PI substrate was soaked in the Tp/n-hexane solution for a certain time, then followed by heat treatment in an oven at 80 °C for 3 min. Afterward, the TpHz-modified PI substrate surface was rinsed using DI water. The concentration of the aqueous Hz solution was fixed at 50.0 mg L−1. The formation mechanism of the TpHz COFs is exhibited in Fig. S1.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fabrication process of the iTFN OSN membrane
ce:section.ce:para: The skin layer of the iTFN OSN membrane followed a similar fabrication process as that of the TpHz-modified PI substrate except that the TpHz-modified PI substrate was served as the substrate, MPD was used as aqueous phase monomer and af-GQDs nanomaterial was used as aqueous phase additive, TMC was used as organic phase monomer. After the IP reaction, the membrane was directly soaked in 10.0 wt% HDA/IPA solution at 60 °C for 30 min to perform chemical crosslinking, and then followed by being soaked in DMF at 80 °C for a certain time to perform solvent activation. Thus, an iTFN OSN membrane with integrally covalent bonding structure was obtained. The preparation process is shown in Fig. 1 . The fabricated iTFN OSN membrane was termed as iTFN-c1-c2-c3-c4, where c1, c2, c3 and c4 are the concentration of MPD, TMC, Tp and af-GQDs, respectively. For comparison, several other composite OSN membranes were also fabricated, among which the ones having neither TpHz interlayer nor doped af-GQDs in the aqueous phase were termed as TFC-c1-c2; the ones having TpHz interlayer but without aqueous phase af-GQDs doping were termed as iTFC-c1-c2-c3; the ones having aqueous phase af-GQDs doping but without TpHz interlayer were termed as TFN-c1-c2-c4.

The reactions that could occur in the preparation process of the iTFN OSN membranes are shown in Fig. 2 . When Hz monomer comes in touch with the PI substrate surface, the imide groups of the PI substrate undergo ring-opening reaction with the –NH2 groups of Hz [15,31]. However, due to the short molecule chain of Hz, once one –NH2 group of Hz reacts with the PI substrate, the other –NH2 group of Hz could be very difficult to reach the PI chain to perform the crosslinking of the PI substrate. So that the free –NH2 groups of Hz could subsequently react with Tp and participate in the reaction to construct the TpHz COFs, and thus covalent bonds could be formed between the generated TpHz COFs interlayer and the PI substrate, which could fix the COFs interlayer firmly on the substrate and greatly enhance its stability during the subsequent long-term use. Further, the generated TpHz COFs interlayer contains a large amount of –NH2 groups, which could react with the -COCl groups of TMC during the subsequent IP reaction [32]. Hence, the formed PA layer could also be fixed firmly to the surface of the TpHz COFs interlayer via covalent bonds, which further enhances the stability of the interlayer and the PA layer. In summary, the TpHz COFs interlayer is covalently bonded to both the separation layer and the PI substrate surface.

Meanwhile, af-GQDs nanomaterials also contain a large amount of –NH2 groups and thus could also react with the -COCl groups of TMC during the IP process [32].That is, af-GQDs could also be firmly incorporated in the separation layer via covalent bonds. As af-GQDs have much small sheet size, they are expected to provide more channels for solvent permeation. In this way, we could construct an iTFN membrane with both nanomaterial interlayer and nanomaterial incorporated skin layer, as well as featuring a wholly covalent bonding three-layer structure, which is expected to have superior separation performance together with favorable long-term stability in organic solvents.
The testing method of solvent permeance (P) and solute rejection (R) was described elsewhere [31], the detail is presented in the supplementary materials.

Pure DMF solvent at 80 °C and 25 °C, as well as pure NMP solvent at 80 °C, respectively, were used to evaluate the resistance to polar solvent of the prepared iTFN membranes. At regular time intervals, the membrane samples were taken out from the solvent, and then their separation performance were measured by a cross-flow filtration platform using 100 mg L−1 RDB/ethanol solution, after which they were immersed back to the solvent.
Long-term cross-flow filtration was also performed to test the solvent resistance of the prepared iTFN membranes using feed solution of 100 mg L−1 RB/DMF at 0.5 MPa and 25 °C.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Surface chemistry of the TFC and iTFN membranes
ce:section.ce:para: Fig. 3 presents the peak fitting of the XPS spectra of the TFC and iTFN membranes, from which the content of the chemical groups on the membrane surface is calculated and is shown in Table 2 . We can see that the iTFN membrane possesses much more O–C=O groups and fewer N–C=O groups compared to the TFC membrane, demonstrating a decrease in the crosslinking degree (from 87.4% to 80.8%) of the PA layer generated by IP process after both introducing the TpHz interlayer and doping the af-GQDs nanomaterials in the aqueous MPD solution. The decrease in crosslinking degree could be attributed to several factors. Due to the steric hindrance of af-GQDs, the diffusion rate of MPD could be decreased, thereby both the reaction speed and reaction degree were reduced, thus retarding the formation of the PA layer [30,33–35]. Meanwhile, both TpHz COFs interlayer and doped af-GQDs nanomaterials have plenty of amine groups, they would surely constitute hydrogen bonding with MPD molecules and hinder the diffusion of MPD towards the interface. Thus, less MPD molecules would reach the interface and participate in the IP reaction, which could surely slow down the reaction speed and reaction degree. Furthermore, the amine groups of TpHz COFs interlayer and af-GQDs could compete with the amine groups of MPD molecules to form amide structure with the acyl chloride groups of TMC [30]. All these effects lead to a decreased crosslinking degree of the PA polymer to some extent. Consequently, the remaining acyl chloride groups of TMC would increase, resulting in the increase of –COOH groups on the membrane surface and the increase of the O–C=O content as well.
For the purpose of decreasing the permeation resistance of the PA layer, we investigated the effect of the MPD concentration on the membrane separation performance.

In general, the reaction degree of the interfacial polymerization would decrease with the decreased MPD concentration, resulting in a thinner PA layer, which is beneficial for faster solvent permeation. However, the skin layer would become smoother with smaller specific surface area, which is disadvantageous for solvent permeance. Whether the solvent permeance would increase or not depends on the balance between these two effects. As shown in Fig. 4 , the ethanol permeance of the prepared iTFN membranes decreases slightly from 52.6 to 49.5 L m−2 h−1 MPa−1 with the decrease of the MPD concentration from 1.0 wt% to 0.5 wt%, which could be due to the effect of much smoother surface over the effect of thinner skin, as can be confirmed by Fig. 5 (a) and (b). In Fig. 5(a), the surface morphology of iTFN-1.0-0.15-25-50 membrane reveals a typical “leaf-like” structure with a rough surface. However, in Fig. 5(b), this structure is significantly weakened, and the surface of the iTFN-0.5-0.15-25-50 membrane becomes flatter and smoother, indicating a smaller specific surface area, thus resulting in a reduced solvent permeance.

As the MPD concentration further decreases from 0.5 wt% to 0.01 wt%, the permeance of ethanol gradually increases from 49.5 to 97.6 L m−2 h−1 MPa−1. We attribute this to the decreased crosslinking degree which is originated from the increased remaining amount of the acryl chloride groups of the TMC monomers on the skin layer with the decreased MPD concentration, as has been clearly illustrated in Section 3.1
. Therefore, on account of the hydrolyzation of these acryl chloride groups to carboxyl groups, the hydrophilicity of the membrane surface will increase, which is an advantage for the increase of the solvent permeation.

As exhibited in Fig. 5(c), the “leaf-like” structure and bulges on the surface of the iTFN-0.025-0.15-25-50 membrane disappear, and the membrane surface is very smooth, which indicates that the use of extremely low concentration MPD and regular concentration TMC could obtain an ultra-smooth surface, this is of great significance for the enhancement of the anti-fouling property of the OSN membranes.

As the further decline of the MPD concentration from 0.025 wt% down to 0.01 wt%, the RDB rejection decreases markedly from 99.2% down to 95.2%. This indicates that at such low MPD concentration, the MPD molecules could not be evenly dispersed on the substrate surface, which leads to the formation of a loose but defective skin layer. Therefore, we select 0.025 wt% as the optimal MPD concentration.
Based upon the selected optimal MPD concentration, we further investigated the influence of TMC concentration on the membrane separation performance.

As shown in Fig. 6 , the RDB rejection remains above 99% and the ethanol permeance gradually increases from 78.4 to 90.7 L m−2 h−1 MPa−1 with the TMC concentration decreases from 0.2 wt% to 0.05 wt%. This could be due to the decrease of solvent transport resistance caused by the decreased thickness of the skin layer performs a predominant role in the influence of membrane permeability [36], as can also be demonstrated by the TEM characterization of the membranes with and without the COFs interlayer, as shown in Fig. 7 . Both the TFN-0.025-0.05-50 and iTFN-0.025-0.05-15-50 membranes have ultra-thin skin layer. The former has a PA skin layer of around 10 nm, which is mainly attributed to the use of extremely low concentration for both MPD and TMC during the IP process. The latter shows a sandwich-like structure, both the TpHz COFs interlayer and the PA skin layer are relatively uniform, and the thickness of the TpHz COFs interlayer and the PA layer is about 5–6 nm and 7–8 nm, respectively. The uniform distribution of the ultra-thin TpHz COFs interlayer is attributed to the use of ultra-low concentration for both Tp and Hz monomers. With the aid of the TpHz COFs interlayer, the PA skin layer thickness of the iTFN-0.025-0.05-15-50 membrane is slightly reduced compared to that of the TFN-0.025-0.05-50 membrane. This is due to the formation of hydrogen bonds between the abundant amine groups on TpHz COFs interlayer and the amine groups on MPD monomers, thus slowing down the diffusion rate of MPD to the interfacial reaction zone and contributing to a thinner PA layer [40]. Therefore, the uniform and ultra-thin TpHz COFs interlayer could effectively regulate the distribution and diffusion of the MPD molecules during the IP process, and is beneficial to generate an extremely thin and smooth PA skin layer.

However, the RDB rejection has a sharp decline down to 89.2% at 0.025 wt% TMC concentration. This indicates that defects form on the skin layer with the TMC concentration going down, so the prepared membrane has poor RDB rejection. Thus, the optimal TMC concentration was selected as 0.05 wt%.

Based on the optimized IP reaction condition of the PA layer, we further studied the influence of the Tp concentration for the COFs interlayer on the separation performance of the iTFN-0.025-0.05-c3-50 membranes, as shown in Fig. 8 . When the Tp concentration is 0, which means that no TpHz interlayer is introduced and corresponds to the TFN-0.025-0.25-50 membrane, the RDB rejection is only 95.2% with a relatively high ethanol permeance of 93.4 L m−2 h−1 MPa−1. This indicates that with such low concentrations for both MPD and TMC, the IP process leads to a relatively loose skin layer with more defects, so that the prepared TFN-0.025-0.25-50 membrane has high ethanol permeance but low RDB rejection. However, at a Tp concentration of 5.0 mg L−1, i.e., TpHz interlayer is introduced, the ethanol permeance sharply drops to 84.9 L m−2 h−1 MPa−1, but the RDB rejection vigorously rises up to 99%. Furthermore, no matter what Tp concentration is used, the RDB rejection of iTFN-0.025-0.25-c3-50 membranes remains essentially at 99%. This indicates that the TpHz interlayer surely takes effect and successfully manipulates the subsequent IP process, contributing to the generation of PA skin layer with both high selectivity and solvent permeance.

With the Tp concentration gradually increases from 5 to 15 mg L−1, the ethanol permeance of the iTFN-0.025-0.25-c3-50 membranes increases from 84.9 to 97.6 L m−2 h−1 MPa−1. This relies on the fact that the hydrophilic TpHz interlayer could promote the uniform distribution of MPD monomers on the membrane surface and could slow down the diffusion of MPD molecules towards the interface, thus helps to form a uniform and ultra-thin PA skin layer, as has been illustrated in Section 3.1 . Hence, the solvent permeance could be effectively increased while ensuring the high solute rejection. However, with the Tp concentration further rises from 15 to 50 mg L−1, the ethanol permeance shows a decline from 97.6 to 87.6 L m−2 h−1 MPa−1. We consider that higher Tp concentration might lead to the formation of much thicker interlayer with excessive stacking of COFs crystals. This is adverse to the uniform distribution of the aqueous phase MPD monomers, and might preserve more aqueous MPD solution, consequently might lead to denser PA layer with higher crosslinking degree, as has been illustrated in Section 3.1 , and thus leads to the decrease in solvent permeance. Therefore, we selected 15 mg L−1 as the optimal Tp concentration for the COFs interlayer.
Fig. 9 and Fig. 10 present the effect of af-GQDs content on the surface roughness and the separation performance of the iTFN-0.025-0.05-15-c4 membranes, respectively.

The membrane prepared with 0 mg L−1 of af-GQDs just corresponds to the iTFC-0.025-0.05-15 membrane, whose surface roughness is only 3.4 nm, as shown in Fig. 9(a), indicating that the introduction of the TpHz interlayer and using extremely low concentrations of MPD and TMC in the interfacial polymerization process are possible to obtain an ultra-smooth separation skin. The surface roughness of the iTFN-0.025-0.05-15-50 membrane prepared with the addition of 50 mg L−1 af-GQDs is 2.7 nm, as presented in Fig. 9(b), which is slightly lower than that of the iTFC-0.025-0.05-15 membrane without the addition of af-GQDs, indicating that appropriate af-GQDs content could help to reduce the surface roughness. As shown in Fig. 9(c), the surface roughness of the iTFN-0.025-0.05-15-100 membrane prepared with the addition of 100 mg L−1 af-GQDs is 3.2 nm, which marginally increases with the doping af-GQDs content increases but is still ultra-smooth.

As shown in Fig. 10, the ethanol permeance exhibits a gradual increase from 74.7 to 97.6 L m−2 h−1 MPa−1 with the doping af-GQDs content rises from 0 to 50 mg L−1. This could be due to the rich amine groups on af-GQDs nanomaterials and the much larger sheet size of af-GQDs than the molecular size of MPD. Therefore, af-GQDs could effectively impede the diffusion of MPD molecules from the aqueous phase solution to the IP reaction interface via both steric resistance and hydrogen bonding, thus reducing the reaction rate and intensity of the IP reaction, and leading to a much thinner PA skin with decreased solvent permeation resistance. This phenomenon has been demonstrated by our previous research work [30]. Furthermore, the lamellar af-GQDs nanomaterials are incorporated in the PA skin layer via covalent bonds between the –NH2 groups of af-GQDs nanomaterials and the -COCl groups of TMC, thus could effectively impede the movement of the polymer chains in the cortex, and make the separation layer form larger free volume between the nanomaterials as well as between the nanomaterial and the polymer [30,37–39]. Therefore, they could provide more confined mass transfer channels for solvent permeation.

However, with the af-GQDs content increases even further from 50 to 200 mg L−1, the ethanol permeance shows a decline from 97.6 to 78.4 L m−2 h−1 MPa−1. At that high content, the af-GQDs nanomaterials could agglomerate [30], and thus could not be able to impede the diffusion of MPD molecules effectively. Hence, more MPD molecules would diffuse towards the interface to react with TMC, resulting in less unreacted acryl chloride groups remaining on the surface of the virgin PA surface. Consequently, less carboxyl groups would remain after the hydrolyzation of the unreacted acryl chloride groups. Therefore, the PA skin layer would become denser with higher crosslinking degree, the mechanism of which is similar to that illustrated in Section 3.1 . All these effects eventually cause the decrease of the solvent permeance. Hence, 50 mg L−1 is the optimal af-GQDs content.

We tested the pure solvent permeance of the iTFN-0.025-0.05-15-50 membrane at 0.50 MPa and 25 °C, respectively. The properties of the pure solvents used in this experiment are listed in Table S1. The factors that affect the membrane permeance to different solvents mainly include the molecular size, polarity and viscosity of the solvent. As presented in Fig. 11 , pure methanol permeance is the highest, which is 220.7 L m−2 h−1 MPa−1, followed by DMF, ethanol, isopropanol, n-hexane and iso-butanol, which are 180.5, 110.5, 18.6, 14.3, 9.5 L m−2 h−1 MPa−1, respectively. Overall, the prepared iTFN-0.025-0.05-15-50 membrane shows higher permeance for polar solvents than nonpolar solvents, and higher permeance for solvents with small molecular size than those with large molecular size. These phenomena could be explained by Table S1. For methanol, DMF and ethanol, which are three polar solvents, the permeance of methanol is the highest, as methanol has the smallest molecular size (about 40.7 cm3 mol−1 in volume and 32 Da in weight) and the lowest viscosity (0.54 × 10−3 Pa s). For DMF and ethanol, although DMF has greater molecular size and viscosity than ethanol, DMF has a greater solubility parameter (the Hansen solubility parameter δ
p,s is 13.7 MPa1/2) and has more similar chemical structure to the PA skin layer than ethanol. So that DMF is more affiliative with the PA layer, and thus the DMF permeance is higher than the ethanol permeance. Isopropanol has larger molecular size (about 76.8 cm3 mol−1 in volume and 60 Da in weight) and viscosity (2.05 × 10−3 Pa s) than other polar solvents, so that it has a low permeance. n-Hexane is nonpolar solvent, and iso-butanol is weak polar solvent, so that they have much low permeance for the hydrophilic iTFN-0.025-0.05-15-50 membrane.

Hansen solubility parameter (δ
p,s ), molecular volume (MV
s ) and dynamic viscosity (η
s ) are commonly used in the form of δ
p,s
MV
s
−1 η
s
−1 [40,41] to correlate the intrinsic solvent properties with the membrane permeance. The above properties of the pure solvents used in this experiment are listed in Table S1. The correlation between the pure solvent permeance and the solvent properties of the iTFN-0.025-0.05-15-50 membrane is shown in Fig. S2. The permeance of pure solvent is closely in proportion to the δ
p,s
MV
s
−1 η
s
−1 value, which indicates that the iTFN-0.025-0.05-15-50 membrane is more permeable to solvents with lower molecular size, lower viscosity and higher polarity.

In order to characterize the MWCO of the prepared iTFN-0.025-0.05-15-50 membrane, PEGs with different molecular weight were used, and the results are shown in Fig. 12 . The 90% rejection of the iTFN-0.025-0.05-15-50 membrane corresponds to PEG molecular weight of about 380 Da.

For comparison, we also tested the separation performance of the iTFN-0.025-0.05-15-50 OSN membrane using several different kinds of dyes, saffron T (ST, 350 Da), rhodamine B (RDB, 479 Da), eosin Y (EY, 647 Da), Kaumas brilliant blue (CBB, 825 Da), and rose Bengal (RB, 1017 Da), respectively, in ethanol, as shown in Fig. S3. The 90% rejection of the iTFN-0.025-0.05-15-50 OSN membrane corresponds to dye molecular weight of 400 Da, which is similar to the MWCO of the membrane tested using PEGs. The MWCO measured by using dyes is a little higher than that by using PEGs, since the dye molecules used are ionic molecules (as has been listed in Table S2), the charged property of the dyes will influence the separation performance of the membranes.

The organic solvent resistance performance of the prepared iTFN-0.025-0.05-15-50 OSN membrane was tested via static immersion method. As presented in Fig. 13 , initially, the iTFN-0.025-0.05-15-50 membrane shows an ethanol permeance of 97.6 L m−2 h−1 MPa−1 and an RDB rejection of 99.2%. After the DMF immersion at 80 °C for 480 h, the ethanol permeance gradually increases to 143.7 L m−2 h−1 MPa−1, while the RDB rejection only slightly decreases to 96.9%. Similar result is also obtained from the NMP immersion test in Fig. S4. As immersion time extends, the separation performance variation trends were similar to those in DMF, the permeance of ethanol gradually increases to 148.5 L m−2 h−1 MPa−1 after NMP immersion at 80 °C for 480 h, while the RDB rejection only slightly decreases to 95.7%.

It should be mentioned that this is a very promising solvent resistance performance for the iTFN-0.025-0.05-15-50 membrane. Till now, seldom any OSN membranes reported in literature works could endure such harsh polar solvent at such high temperature, except those in our previous works [16,29,30,42,43]. This indicates that the fabricated iTFN-0.025-0.05-15-50 membrane has superior solvent resistance performance under extreme harsh polar solvent environments, even at higher temperature, which can greatly expand its potential application in these environments.
The long-time filtration test of the prepared iTFN-0.025-0.05-15-50 membrane using 100 mg L−1 of RB/DMF solution was presented in Fig. 14 .

From Fig. 14, within the first 30 h, the DMF permeance gradually increases from 153 to 161 L m−2 h−1 MPa−1, which relies on the further solvent activation of the iTFN-0.025-0.05-15-50 membrane by DMF [44,45]. After that, the DMF permeance gradually decreases from 161 to 145 L m−2 h−1 MPa−1 at 180 h. This phenomenon could be principally resulted from the dyes enriched on the membrane surface during the long-time filtration process. Overall, the prepared iTFN-0.025-0.05-15-50 membrane shows a slight reduction of only about 5.2% for the DMF permeance while the RB rejection remains always above 99%, indicating that the prepared iTFN-0.025-0.05-15-50 OSN membrane has excellent long-time filtration stability performance.

Table 3 presents the separation performance of the OSN membranes we achieved and those of others reported in the recent literatures. The prepared iTFN OSN membrane in this work not only has high solvent permeance but also has high solute rejection. Although a few OSN membranes such as the MWCNTs-COOH/P84 membrane [46] and the HPEI-GO/PP membrane [47] also have high ethanol permeance, but they have relatively low dye rejection. Comparatively, the iTFN OSN membrane prepared in this work has not only excellent separation performance, but also long-term solvent resistance stability due to its integrally covalent bonding structure, as well as excellent fouling resistance due to its ultra-smooth surface. In summary, the comparison result indicates that the prepared iTFN membrane possesses broad application prospects in organic solvents treatment.

In this work, a novel integrally covalent and nanomaterials-interlayered thin-film nanocomposite (iTFN) OSN membrane was preciously constructed. This iTFN OSN membrane features both TpHz COFs interlayer and af-GQDs nanoparticles incorporation in the skin layer. These two kinds of nanomaterials were fully utilized to reduce the diffusion rate of MPD into the IP reaction zone, thus effectively regulating the IP process and the microstructure of the formed PA layer, resulting in an iTFN OSN membrane with ultra-thin and ultra-smooth skin layer, integrally covalent bonding structure, confined mass transfer function, and high separation performance. The thickness of the interlayer and the separation layer is about 5–6 nm and 7–8 nm, respectively, under optimal conditions. The ethanol permeance reaches 97.6 L m−2 h−1 MPa−1 while the RDB rejection remains above 99%. The iTFN OSN membrane has superior solvent resistance performance, it could remain higher than 95% RDB rejection after the immersion treatment of DMF at 80 °C for 480 h, and could remain higher than 99% RB rejection after the long-term cross flow filtration with 100 mg L−1 RB/DMF solution for up to 180 h, indicating its broad application prospects in organic solutions treatment.

All the authors listed has approved the manuscript for publication. Shuxuan Li: Conceptualization, Data curation, Writing - reviewing & editing. Yating Yin: Methodology, Writing - manuscript preparation, reviewing & editing, Shaoxiao Liu: Writing - reviewing & editing. Honghai Li: Writing - reviewing & editing. Baowei Su: Conceptualization, Methodology, Writing - reviewing & editing, Supervision, Funding acquisition, Project administration. Lihui Han: Methodology, Writing - review & editing. Xueli Gao: Methodology, Writing - reviewing & editing. Congjie Gao: Supervision, Methodology.

The work described was original and has not been published previously, and not under consideration for publication elsewhere, in whole or in part, and no conflict of interest exits in the submission of this manuscript.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by National Natural Science Foundation of China (No. 22078310).
